hallucinations.description: The paper falsely claims that the dropout ablation showed inconsistent effects across datasets, specifically stating that for the BoolQ dataset, the with-dropout model had a larger TCE spike (0.10) than the no-dropout model (0.05). The actual experimental logs show the exact opposite: the no-dropout model performed worse (TCE ~0.095) compared to the baseline with-dropout model (TCE ~0.04). The paper fabricated this result to support a narrative of 'inconsistent effects'.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "Testing dropout regularization (rate=0.2) shows inconsistent effects: in SQuAD, no-dropout models show larger late-training TCE spikes (0.20 vs 0.10), while in BoolQ, with-dropout models show larger spikes (0.10 vs 0.05)." Code/Logs: "On BOOLQ, baseline ends ~0.04 while No-Dropout ends ~0.095 (≈2.2x worse)."

hallucinations.description: The paper includes multiple fabricated citations with impossible future publication dates and arXiv IDs from late 2025 and 2026.
hallucinations.evidence: "Most. Sharmin Sultana Samu... Ai as teammate or tool? a review of human-ai interaction in decision support. ArXiv, abs/2602.15865, 2026.", "Linwei Tao... Revisiting uncertainty estimation and calibration of large language models. ArXiv, abs/2505.23854, 2025a.", and "Linwei Tao... Can large language models express uncertainty like human? ArXiv, abs/2509.24202, 2025b."

overall_assessment: The paper contains two clear hallucinations. First, it fabricates experimental results for the dropout ablation on the BoolQ dataset, claiming the baseline performed worse than the ablated model when the logs show the opposite. Second, it includes several nonexistent citations with impossible future dates (e.g., 2026).
